result.markdown:
--- title: Lab Four -- Programming in R # Title of the document # Bibliography Details bibliography: Lab4References.bib # Name of bib file in the same folder bibliographystyle: apa # Bibliography style execute: # Default settings for code blocks warning: false # Do not print warnings message: false # Do not print messages size: "scriptsize" # Set code size to script size # Below will do html by default. To render the entire project # Use the following code: # quarto::quarto_render("Lab4WriteUp.qmd") format: html: # Specify HTML formatting options number-sections: true # Number Sections code-fold: true # Button to show code code-line-numbers: true # Line numbers printed in code cap-location: top # Captions at the top of tables and figures pdf: # Specify .pdf formatting options documentclass: article # Document Class number-sections: true # Number Sections reference-section-title: "References" # Title for reference section code-line-numbers: true # Line numbers printed in code cap-location: top # Captions at the top of tables geometry: - top=0.75in - left=0.75in - bottom=0.75in - right=0.75in format-links: [pdf] --- \normalsize ::: {.cell} ::: \normalsize * Complete the tasks below. Make sure to start your solutions in on a new line that starts with "**Solution**:". * Make sure to use the Quarto Cheatsheet. This will make completing and writing up the lab *much* easier. In this lab, we will build a mealkit recipe generator. I created a small website of about 40 recipes. We will scrape recipes from that website, randomly select three meals, and print a grocery list with recipe cards. # Preliminaries ## Part a Below, I create a numeric vector filled with random observations. Ask for the 3rd item.
**Note**: The `set.seed(7272)` portion ensures we all get the same answer. \scriptsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} set.seed(7272) # sets the randomization seed for replication x <- sample(x=1:10, # sample from 1, 2, 3, ..., 10 size=10, # sample of 10 replace = TRUE) # allowed to sample the same item multiple times ``` ::: \normalsize **Solution** \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} x[3] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 5 ``` ::: ::: \normalsize ## Part b Below, I create a data frame filled with random observations. \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} set.seed(7272) # sets the randomization seed for replication df <- data.frame(x1 = sample(x=1:10, # sample from 1, 2, 3, ..., 10 size=10, # sample of 10 replace = TRUE), # allowed to sample the same item multiple times x2 = sample(x=1:10, # sample from 1, 2, 3, ..., 10 size=10, # sample of 10 replace = TRUE), # allowed to sample the same item multiple times x3 = sample(x=1:10, # sample from 1, 2, 3, ..., 10 size=10, # sample of 10 replace = TRUE) # allowed to sample the same item multiple times, ) ``` ::: \normalsize ## Part b Use the `head(...)` function to peek at the data frame. **Solution** \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} head(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` x1 x2 x3 1 8 1 8 2 3 7 2 3 5 3 3 4 3 6 2 5 2 2 3 6 3 8 10 ``` ::: ::: \normalsize ## Part c Ask for the column `x1`. **Solution** The following code gets the column named x1. \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df$x1 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 8 3 5 3 2 3 1 2 6 2 ``` ::: ::: \normalsize ## Part d Ask for the fifth row of the data frame. **Solution** The following code gets the fifth row of the data frame, which includes all columns. \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df[5,] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` x1 x2 x3 5 2 2 3 ``` ::: ::: \normalsize ## Part e Ask for the the value of `x1` in the fifth row of the data frame. **Solution** This code returns the value of x1 in the fifth row of the data frame. \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df$x1[5] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 2 ``` ::: ::: \normalsize ## Part f Ask for the the value of in the third column and the fifth row of the data frame. **Solution** The below code returns the value in the third column and fifth row. \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df[5, 3] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 3 ``` ::: ::: \normalsize ## Part g Create a sequence from 1 to 10 by 2 and use it to print the odd rows of the data frame. **Solution** The below code creates a vector odds, and then prints the odd rows of the data frame. \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} odds=seq(from=1, to=10, by =2) (df[odds,]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` x1 x2 x3 1 8 1 8 3 5 3 3 5 2 2 3 7 1 10 1 9 6 2 10 ``` ::: ::: \normalsize ## Part h Below, I create an empty column called `x12`. Fill in the details of the `for(...)` loop to ensure `x12` is the product of `x1` and `x2`. **Solution** \scriptsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} n <- nrow(df) # How many rows to we have to fill? df$x12 <- rep(NA, nrow(df)) # Create an empty column for x12 for(i in 1:nrow(df)){ df$x12[i]=df$x1[i]*df$x2[i] } ``` ::: \normalsize ## Part i Write a function called `calculate.score(...)` that takes three arguments representing `x1`, `x2`, and `x3`) and returns a single value based on the formula: $$Score = (x_1 \times 2) + x_2 - x_3$$ Use your function to create a new column `total.score` in our data frame `df`. **Solution** \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} calculate.score = function(x1, x2, x3){ x1*2+x2-x3 } n <- nrow(df) df$total.score <- rep(NA, nrow(df)) for(i in 1:nrow(df)){ df$total.score[i]=calculate.score(df$x1[i], df$x2[i], df$x3[i]) } df$total.score ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 9 11 10 10 3 4 11 9 4 7 ``` ::: ::: \normalsize ## Part j Create a function called `evaluate.row(...)` that takes one argument and returns "low" when the argument is less than 4, "mid" when the argument is between 4 and 7 (inclusive), and "high" when the arguement is 8 or larger. Then, use `sapply(...)` to apply it to column `x1`. **Solution** \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} evaluate.row = function(x){ if(x < 4){ return("low") } else if(x <= 7){ return("mid") } else if (x >= 8){ return("high") } } sapply(df$x1, evaluate.row) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "high" "low" "mid" "low" "low" "low" "low" "low" "mid" "low" ``` ::: ::: \normalsize ## Part k Did we need to use loops or functions in (h.)-(j.)? That is, can we use vectorization to attain the same results in 1 line each? Where it is possible, write the line of code. Where it is not, explain why. **Solution** For part h, it is possible to multiply the columns together to get the same result as using a loop. \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df$test=df$x1*df$x2 ``` ::: \normalsize For part i, a similar thing can be done to part h to get it done in one line. \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df$total.score.test=2*df$x1+df$x2-df$x3 ``` ::: \normalsize For part j, it is possible to use nested if else statements as demonstrated below. \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sapply(df$x1, function(x) ifelse(x < 4, "low", ifelse(x <= 7, "mid", "high"))) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "high" "low" "mid" "low" "low" "low" "low" "low" "mid" "low" ``` ::: ::: \normalsize # Complete Tasks for One Recipe ## Part a Install and load the `rvest` package [@rvest]. **Solution** \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # install.packages('rvest') library(rvest) ``` ::: \normalsize ## Part b Load the html of the `website/KimchiGrilledCheese.html` using the `read_html()` function. We will use the Kimchi Grilled Cheese recipe as our prototype and extend this workflow to all recipes, so try to be as general as possible. If you do look at it, you'll notice it contains the html we saw in the developer tools in class.
**Hint**: You can use `read_html(...)` like `read.csv(...)`. Don't forget you can use the documentation to help use it. **Solution** \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} grilled.cheese=read_html('Lab 4/Lab 4/website/KimchiGrilledCheese.html') ``` ::: \normalsize ## Part c Open the html file in a web browser and open developer tools. In chrome-based browsers, you can do this by pressing the three verticle dots in the upper-right corner, clicking "more tools", then "developer tools". Find the name of the Ingredients section of the website and pull the HTML of the function using `html_element(...)` and save the results to an object called `ingredients.section`.
**Hint**: You can ask for elements by id using a preceeding "\#". See `?html_element(...)` for a helpful example. **Solution** \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ingredients.section=html_element(grilled.cheese, "#ingredients") ``` ::: \normalsize ## Part d Now, we want to obtain all of the itemized items. Find the element type the individual ingredients and pull all of them from `ingredients.section` using `html_elements(...)` (note the added s) and save the results to an object called `ingredients`.
**Hint**: You can ask for elements by type by simply specifying the tag (e.g., "p" for paragraph). See `?html_element for a helpful example. **Solution** \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} html_elements(ingredients.section, '*') ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` {xml_nodeset (11)} [1]

Ingredients

[2]
    \n
  • 1/8 tsp chili flakes
    • \n
  • 4 garlic cloves
    • \n
  • 5 oz ... [3]
  • 1/8 tsp chili flakes
    • [4]
  • 4 garlic cloves
    • [5]
  • 5 oz spinach
    • [6]
  • 1 tsp gochujang
    • [7]
  • 3 tsp mayonnaise
    • [8]
  • 2/3 cup kimchi
    • [9]
  • 4 slices of bread
    • [10]
  • 2/3 cup cheddar cheese
    • [11]
  • 4 tablespoons everything seasoning.
    • ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} ingredients=html_elements(ingredients.section, 'li') ``` ::: \normalsize ## Part e Similar to Part c. Find the name of the Instructions section of the website and pull the HTML of the function using `html_element(...)` and save the results to an object called `instructions.section`.
    **Solution** \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} instructions.section=html_element(grilled.cheese, "#instructions") ``` ::: \normalsize ## Part f Now, we want to obtain all of the enumerated items. Find the element type the individual instructions and pull all of them from `instructions.section` using `html_elements(...)` (note the added s) and save the results to an object called `instructions`. **Solution** \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} html_elements(instructions.section, '*') ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` {xml_nodeset (7)} [1]

    Instructions

    [2]
      \n
    1. In a frying pan, heat 2 tablespoons olive oil with chi ... [3]
    2. In a frying pan, heat 2 tablespoons olive oil with chili flakes.
      3. [4]
    3. Add spinach and cook until wilted.
      4. [5]
    4. Mix gochujang and mayonnaise in a small bowl and spread on one side o ... [6]
    5. Build sandwiches by layering spinach, kimchi, and cheese between two ... [7]
    6. Fry in butter until golden brown on both sides.
      7. ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} instructions=html_elements(instructions.section, 'li') ``` ::: \normalsize ## Part g Find the class of the recipe image and pull the HTML using `html_element(...)` and save the results to an object called `image.element`.
      **Hint**: You can ask for elements by class using a preceeding ".". See `?html_element(...)` for a helpful example. Further, note that HTML elements may have more than one class (separated by a space). When that is the case, you need to choose one. **Solution** \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} image.element=html_element(grilled.cheese, '.img-fluid') ``` ::: \normalsize ## Part h Use the `html_attr(...)` function to pull the source link ("src"). Then, use `paste(...)` to prepend the source link with "website/" so we have the full link.
      **Note**: This would be like adding "https://www.website.com" to get the absolute link. **Solution** \normalsize ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} source.link=html_attr(image.element, "src") image.url=paste("Lab 4/Lab 4/website/", source.link, sep="") ``` ::: \normalsize ## Part i Now that we have all the things we need, let's try to print the recipe. Below, I have written code to print from the objects. One by one, remove `#| eval: false` from the YAML header and add `#| echo: false` and `#| results: 'asis'`, and test. Let me know if you're stuck! **Image** \normalsize \normalsize **Ingredients** \normalsize - 1/8 tsp chili flakes - 4 garlic cloves - 5 oz spinach - 1 tsp gochujang - 3 tsp mayonnaise - 2/3 cup kimchi - 4 slices of bread - 2/3 cup cheddar cheese - 4 tablespoons everything seasoning. \normalsize **Instructions** \normalsize 1. In a frying pan, heat 2 tablespoons olive oil with chili flakes. 1. Add spinach and cook until wilted. 1. Mix gochujang and mayonnaise in a small bowl and spread on one side of each slice of bread. Sprinkle with everything seasoning and press it into the bread. 1. Build sandwiches by layering spinach, kimchi, and cheese between two slices of bread. 1. Fry in butter until golden brown on both sides. \normalsize # Complete a Full Menu! Open `Menu.qmd` and add code to complete the following. 1. Randomly select three dinner recipes and one breakfast recipe at random.
      **Hint:** Use the `sample(...)` function. 2. Pull the image, ingredients, and instructions for each selected recipe. 3. Combine all of the ingredients into a grocery list on the first page. 4. Print the image, ingredients, and instructions for each receipe on the subsequent pages. When you render this document, no code should be visible. Instead, you should see a five-page document as described above. # Describe your work! ## Why a fake website? Read [https://www.scrapingbee.com/blog/is-web-scraping-legal/](Is Web Sraping Legal?). Originally, I conceived doing this with recipes from Purple Carrot (my favorite subscription service). Being a large company, their terms of service are *very* long and precluded us from copying their recipes. We also checked a few smaller recipe websites we like and even they had terms of service that restricted automated collection of data. **Solution** There are specific no web scraping laws in the US, however there have been court cases debating this subject and subsequent rulings that set precedents for future cases, such as hiQ labs vs. LinkedIn. Many websites have terms of service that prevent you from scraping them. Also, some states like California and Virginia have their own laws regarding web scraping of personal information. Therefore, making a fake website means that we do not run into any of these legal issues and are free to scrape it as much as we want. ## Conditionals Did you use conditional statements in your code? If yes, how. If not, are there places you could have used them but did something else? **Solution** I did not use any conditionals. I could have used conditionals to randomly sample meals from the list of available ones and thus randomize the order of how the meals appeared on the menu. Instead, I used the sample function. ## Loops Did you use loops in your code? If yes, how. If not, are there places you could have used them but did something else? **Solution** I did not use loops in my code. I could have looped over the different recipies, but instead I copy and pasted the code three times. ## Functions Did you use functions in your code? If yes, how. If not, are there places you could have used them but did something else? **Solution** I did not use any functions. I could have created a function for grabbing the instructions from the html, but instead I copy pasted my code each time.
